exemplar.title: An investigation of the Mere Exposure Effect on music

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation of the Mere Exposure Effect on music

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim clearly stated with coherent rationale linking to everyday music exposure
Theory/model described and explicitly connected to the investigation
Variables operationalized with clear null and research hypotheses
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in theoristâs name and original study description
Hypothesis section has a truncated sentence and could integrate operational definitions more concisely
Spelling and terminological errors (e.g., âobligated attractionâ) detract from credibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the descriptor for band 5â6: the aim and relevance are clearly articulated; the theory/model is described and linked; and variables are fully operationalized. However, recurring minor errors (spelling of âZajone,â truncated phrasing, slight mischaracterization of Zajoncâs findings) prevent a perfect score of 6, placing the Introduction at the bottom of the top band (5/6).

strengths: Design choice (repeated measures) is explained beyond mere description
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are identified with some rationale
Controlled variables and materials are listed with brief justifications
weaknesses: No counterbalancing or order effects are addressed in the design
Volume control lacks standardization, introducing possible variability
Convenience sampling bias and materialâchoice rationales are only superficially explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each Exploration strand shows explanation rather than mere description, aligning with band 3â4. However, omissionsâcounterbalancing, deeper discussion of sampling bias, and rationale for materialsâlimit depth, so a midâband score of 3/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Appropriate choice of descriptive and inferential statistics (means, SDs, Wilcoxon)
Graph addresses hypothesis rather than being purely aesthetic
Findings are interpreted with reference to hypothesis, rejecting Hâ
weaknesses: Inferential calculation error (critical value computed for N=16, not 19)
Graph lacks labels, units, and error bars, undermining clarity
Median/IQR omitted despite ordinal data, and pâvalue is not reported exactly
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods are appropriate but contain critical calculation errors (band 3â4), and the graphical presentation has multiple flaws, placing these two strands firmly in the 3â4 band. Although interpretation is strong (band 5â6), two of three strands are weaker, resulting in an overall Analysis score of 3/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with explicit reference to schema theory and mereâexposure literature
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and tied to validity and generalizability
Modifications are clearly linked to identified limitations and fully justified
weaknesses: Some limitations (e.g., social-media exposure) lack explicit linkage to validity
Practical feasibility and ethical considerations of proposed modifications are not discussed
Engagement with alternative explanations or broader literature could be deeper
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Evaluation strands satisfy the 5â6 descriptors: findings are discussed in theoretical context, strengths/limitations are relevant and explained, and modifications are well-justified. Minor omissions regarding feasibility and ethics prevent a full 6, so this criterion receives 5/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of means is provided and addresses the hypothesis, yet axes lack clear labels/units and no dispersion/error bars are shown. These flaws constitute âerrorsâ per the rubric, so band 3-4 is appropriate, with a score of 3.

The student states statistical findings (means, SDs, Wilcoxon result) and explicitly interprets them in relation to the data and the hypothesis, concluding that the null hypothesis is rejected. Interpretation is coherent and correctly linked to the investigation aims, justifying band 5-6 (bottom of band because of earlier statistical error).

Both IV (song popularity as a proxy for exposure) and DV (self-reported preference on a 1-10 scale) are stated and operationalised. Null and research hypotheses are provided using correct notation. Operational definitions specify how popularity and preference are to be measured. This fulfils the requirements for band 5-6.

Sampling technique (convenience) and participant characteristics (19 students, age 16, same class) are provided, with a brief rationale regarding ease and assumed responsibility. This explanation meets the 3-4 band, though representativeness and bias are only lightly discussed, keeping the score to the lower end of that band.

The student outlines Zajoncâs original mere-exposure study and summarises schema theory, then explicitly connects both constructs to the present investigation (repeated exposure to popular vs. less-popular songs and the adaptation of music-preference schemas). The theory is described with only minor inaccuracies and the link to the investigation is clearly explained, meeting band 5-6.

Results are discussed through schema theory and mere-exposure literature, not merely described. The student explains how the pattern of ratings supports theoretical expectations. This meets the descriptor for âdiscussed with referenceâ, so band 5-6 with a score of 5.

The aim is clearly stated (to investigate the role of the mere-exposure effect in music preference) and the student provides a brief rationale that situates the study in everyday exposure to music and the importance of preference formation. The explanation of why this aim is relevant (ubiquity of music, possible manipulation of preferences) is explicit and coherent, satisfying the descriptor for band 5-6.

Several strengths (control of extraneous variables, repeated measures) and limitations (social-media exposure assumption, small homogeneous sample) are explained and explicitly linked to how they impact validity/generalizability, satisfying band 5-6.

A list of controlled variables (age, gender balance, volume, identical scales, same artist) is given, each with a short justification of control. Materials are also enumerated. This constitutes an explanation, matching band 3-4, but depth is limited, so a mid-band score is appropriate.

The student proposes concrete changes (extended exposure over a week, volunteer sampling) and directly ties each to identified limitations (convenience bias, uncontrolled prior exposure). The justifications are clear and thorough, fulfilling the highest descriptor, hence band 5-6 with a score of 5.

The student identifies the repeated-measures design and explains why it was chosen (fewer participants, control of individual differences). This constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, aligning with band 3-4. The account lacks depth on potential order effects or counterbalancing, so the top of the band is not fully justified.

Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) and an inferential test (Wilcoxon) are applied and appropriate for ordinal paired data. However, the critical value is derived for N = 16 instead of 19, indicating a calculation error. This places the work in band 3-4 where appropriate statistics are applied but contain errors.

annotations.comment: Control of age, gender, scales, and artist is comprehensive and clearly justified, enhancing internal validity.
Volume control is described as âadjusted to participantsâ liking,â which introduces variability. Specify a standardized volume level to ensure consistency.
Descriptive analysis justifies using the mean but overlooks medianâs robustness for ordinal data. Consider reporting median and interquartile range.
Graph 1 lacks axis labels, units, and error bars. Enhance the figure by adding clear labels and visualizing variability to address the hypothesis robustly.
Interpretation in Section 3.1 suggests confirmation without inferential support. Move discussion of significance to the inferential section.
Table 1 presents means and SDs but misses median and IQR for ordinal scales. Including these would provide a fuller view of data distribution.
The critical value for the Wilcoxon test is computed for N=16 but your sample is N=19. Recalculate using the correct sample size to avoid inferential error.
The limitation regarding social media exposure is noted but does not explicitly link to the validity of preference measures. Clarify this connection.
Convenience sampling limitation is described but could expand on its effect on external validity and how volunteer sampling addresses this.
The schema theory connection is articulated but the spelling of âZajoneâ should be corrected to âZajonc.â Ensure all theoristsâ names are accurate to maintain credibility.
The hypothesis section lists IV and DV but defers operational definitions. Integrate concise operationalization here (e.g. stream counts, rating scales) to improve clarity.

The description of Zajonc et al. (1968) refers to âobligated attraction,â which mischaracterizes the observed effect of increased liking. Revise to accurately state that repeated exposure led to higher likability ratings.
The final sentence is truncated (âdriving toâ). Complete the thought to clearly convey how everyday exposure contexts make music a suitable stimulus.
Operational definitions on page 5 effectively clarify measurement of song popularity and preference. This strengthens hypothesis operationalization.
Include the exact p-value for the Wilcoxon test rather than reporting p<0.05 to convey the strength of the evidence.
Discussion properly links findings to schema theory but could engage more critically with alternative explanations or literature beyond Zajonc.
Strengths and limitations are well articulated and linked to validity; consider tabulating them for clearer presentation.
Proposed modifications are justified but feasibility and resource constraints are not considered. Briefly discuss practical implementation.
Volunteer sampling is proposed but ethical considerations (e.g., self-selection bias, informed consent) should be addressed.
The aim is clearly stated and its relevance to everyday music exposure is sound, framing the investigation well.
Consider including recent studies on mere exposure in musical contexts to deepen the theoretical background and relevance.
Use LaTeX for hypothesis notation: write research hypothesis as $$H_1$$ and null as $$H_0$$ to meet academic conventions.
Materials are clearly listed but lack rationale for each choice. Explain why, for example, rating scales from 1â10 and specific songs were selected.
Convenience sampling is described but bias and limited representativeness arenât fully explained. Discuss how this impacts generalizability.
The repeated-measures design is justified for efficiency but order effects and counterbalancing are not addressed. Include this to strengthen design validity.
Procedure is detailed but plays high-frequency song first consistently. Suggest counterbalancing order to control practice or fatigue effects.
